beats.text: The most common reason a fine-tune disappoints is not the algorithm, it is the data you feed it.

visual.sub: Every example must earn its place

beats.text: Four checks do most of the work in order: deduplicate, decontaminate, quality filter, then balance the set.
Tagging turns a flat pile of text into a steerable signal by labeling each example with its source, domain, and quality.
Effective diversity is the exponential of the entropy of the source mixture, not the raw count of sources.
A corpus that is ninety percent one source and ten percent another has an effective diversity near one point three eight.
Read fifty of your own examples by hand before training, since the cheapest quality filter is a pair of eyes.

visual.label: examples to read by hand first

beats.text: A transformer's layers do not all do the same job, so where you draw the freeze line is a high-leverage knob.
Freezing a block excludes its parameters from the optimizer, so they keep pre-trained values and need no optimizer state.
Freezing the first k of L blocks leaves a trainable count of L minus k blocks plus the output head.
Freezing twenty-four of thirty-two equal blocks leaves exactly one quarter of the parameters trainable.
Trainable parameters and the optimizer memory they demand fall as you freeze more of the backbone.
Gradual unfreezing thaws layers top-down over time, each with a smaller learning rate than the one above it.
Instruction fine-tuning teaches a model how to behave, but it does not, by itself, teach it a new domain.

visual.sub: Adapt the base before you specialize it

beats.text: Continued pre-training keeps the next-token objective running, now on a large corpus of in-domain raw text.
The first arrow moves the model's distribution onto your domain, the second teaches the actual skill.
Use a fraction of the original pre-training peak learning rate, since too high overwrites general knowledge.
Mix a slice of general text back into the domain corpus, or you get a model that has forgotten normal conversation.
Reach for it when held-out perplexity on your domain is high, signaling the model has not read enough of your world.

visual.label: epochs of domain text, standard shape

beats.text: Two questions about order and proportion remain: in what sequence and in what ratio do examples arrive.

visual.sub: Order and proportion shape the optimizer's view

beats.text: Presenting examples easy first and hard later can speed convergence and reach better optima than random shuffling.
A mixed objective weights the new domain by one minus r and the old replay corpus by the replay fraction r.

visual.note: r in [0,1] is the replay fraction

beats.text: A small replay fraction, often one to ten percent, recovers most retained capability for a small slowdown.
Replay is the single most reliable defense against forgetting because old gradients stay partly alive.
Skewing the new mix toward one source collapses effective diversity even though the source count is unchanged.
Catastrophic forgetting is the tendency of a network trained on task B to overwrite the weights that encoded task A.

visual.sub: The failure mode the whole chapter circles

beats.text: It is not a bug in the optimizer, the loss on B says nothing about A so updates can freely wreck A.
Forgetting is the gap between old-task score before and after, with retention as the ratio of the two.
A model dropping from ninety to fifty-five percent on a benchmark has lost thirty-five accuracy points.
Mitigations rise in cost: replay, parameter-efficient fine-tuning, lower learning rates, then EWC regularizers.
Elastic Weight Consolidation adds a quadratic penalty that makes important weights stiff and free ones movable.
